coredata.dc:title: Waste derived approach towards wealthy fluorescent N-doped graphene quantum dots for cell imaging and H2O2 sensing applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Khose, Rahul V.
Bangde, Prachi
Bondarde, Mahesh P.
Dhumal, Pratik S.
Bhakare, Madhuri A.
Chakraborty, Goutam
Ray, Alok K.
Dandekar, Prajakta
Some, Surajit
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the synthesis of a highly fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) from waste precursors such as melamine sponge and arjuna bark via a microwave treatment and its functional and morphological characterization using various spectroscopy techniques such as optical, FTIR, XPS and TEM. The as-prepared aqueous N-GQD (dia. 2–3 nm) was used for the bio-imaging application using breast carcinoma cell line (MDA-MB-231) as a model, and the locations of all cells in the cytoplasm as well as nuclei were observed to stain brightly in blue fluorescent color successfully. In addition to that, the aqueous N-GQD showed fluorescence quenching behavior in the presence of hydrogen peroxide, which was exploited to sense H2O2, a probable toxin generated in the diseased cells. Importantly, the cell cytotoxicity was measured and found to be non-toxic (70% survival) to the MDA-MB-231 cells even at very high concentration (∼1.8 mg/ml) of the synthesized N-GQD. This study revealing excellent biocompatibility and imaging of the model cancer cells, and sensing of H2O2 by fluorescent quenching, indicates potential in-vivo cell culture applications of the prepared fluorescent N-GQD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQD
Bio-derived sources
Microwave synthesis
Cell toxicity
Bioimaging

Fluorescence of N-GQD at physiological pH conditions
Fluorescence of N-GQD at cell culture environment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by DAE-BRNS research grant fund (34/14/14/2015/BRNS) and CSIR (22/0748/17/EMR-II), and Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research, Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune. R.V.K is thankful to DAE-BRNS for providing research fellowship. The authors are thankful to laboratory supports of the host institutes ICT and BARC, Mumbai, India.

This work was supported by DAE-BRNS research grant fund (34/14/14/2015/BRNS) and CSIR (22/0748/17/EMR-II), and Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research, Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune. R.V.K is thankful to DAE-BRNS for providing research fellowship. The authors are thankful to laboratory supports of the host institutes ICT and BARC, Mumbai, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of the prepared N-GQD.
(a) XPS survey spectra of N-GQD, and its corresponding (b) C1s spectra, (c) O1s spectra, (d) N1s spectra.
TEM analysis of the prepared N-GQD.

UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the aqueous N-GQD at buffer PBS (pH 7.4). Emission spectrum was recorded at excitation wavelength 405 nm. The similar emission spectrum was obtained at any excitation wavelength within 395 nm to 405 nm.
Emission spectra of aqueous N-GQD (concentration 1 mg/ml) at different pH (4–9) conditions. Blank fluorescence spectrum is at pH 7.4.

(a) Modulations in the fluorescence spectra of the aqueous N-GQD with progressive addition of H2O2 (0.1 M to 1 M). The pH of N-GQD solution is 7.4, and corresponding (b) linear regression plot to obtain limit of detection (LOD) for sensing of aqueous H2O2 using observed fluorescence quenching of the N-GQD. Calculated LOD is 3.3*σ/S = 3.3*2562/39810 = 0.212 M.
Percentage cell survivability of the model MDA MB 231 cells after incubation with different concentrations of the N-GQD (mg/ml) for a period of 24 h, determined by an MTT assay study.

In vitro fluorescence images of the MDA MB 231 cells with the intrinsic photoluminescence of the prepared N-GQD (1 mg/ml) after incubation for a period of 24 h, (a) bright-field image, (b) fluorescence image, and (c) merged image. Fluorescence images were recorded by the laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy under excitation at 405 nm.
Synthesis of N-GQD from arjuna bark and waste melamine sponge. Also, shows bright blue luminescent N-GQDs under the illumination of UV light.

head.ce:title: Waste derived approach towards wealthy fluorescent N-doped graphene quantum dots for cell imaging and H2O2 sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser and Plasma Technology Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: RRF, Laser and Plasma Technology Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: RRF, Laser and Plasma Technology Division

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: RRF, Laser and Plasma Technology Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present article is based on Synthesis of N-GQD by using waste material such as arjuna bark and melamine sponge, which is used for in vitro as well as in vivo cells imaging and H2O2 sensing in biological systems.

Herein, we report the synthesis of a highly fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) from waste precursors such as melamine sponge and arjuna bark via a microwave treatment and its functional and morphological characterization using various spectroscopy techniques such as optical, FTIR, XPS and TEM. The as-prepared aqueous N-GQD (dia. 2–3 nm) was used for the bio-imaging application using breast carcinoma cell line (MDA-MB-231) as a model, and the locations of all cells in the cytoplasm as well as nuclei were observed to stain brightly in blue fluorescent color successfully. In addition to that, the aqueous N-GQD showed fluorescence quenching behavior in the presence of hydrogen peroxide, which was exploited to sense H2O2, a probable toxin generated in the diseased cells. Importantly, the cell cytotoxicity was measured and found to be non-toxic (70% survival) to the MDA-MB-231 cells even at very high concentration (∼1.8 mg/ml) of the synthesized N-GQD. This study revealing excellent biocompatibility and imaging of the model cancer cells, and sensing of H2O2 by fluorescent quenching, indicates potential in-vivo cell culture applications of the prepared fluorescent N-GQD.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, the most abundant and important element for sustaining animal and plant life cycles, has been blessing human civilization repeatedly through revelation of different carbon allotropes such as graphite, diamond, fullerene, and lately in the form of graphene in 2004 [1-3]. Subsequently, over a period of time, each of these inventions, including graphene has been utilized as a valuable resource by scientists and technologists, followed by a rapid exploration of their distinctive and spectacular properties. The wonder nanomaterial 2-dimensional (2D) graphene is made up of single bond and single atom, sp2 bonded carbon atoms, but pristine graphene material is a zero-bandgap semiconductor, which limits its applications in optoelectronic fields since it provides no optical luminescence [4]. Further efforts laid down different routes for preparing sheets of zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which have been revealing promising properties of both graphene, comprising of monolayer or a few tens of layers of carbon flakes, and quantum dot (QD) with properties of nanocrystal semiconductors such as quantum confinement, edge effects leading to size dependent band gaps with intrinsic photoluminescence (PL) and other unique characteristics. Moreover, in comparison to widely used photoluminescent inorganic semiconductor QDs and fluorescent dyes, GQDs are relatively non-toxic, eco-friendly and photostable. Due to such interesting and useful properties, GQDs have been exploited for diverse scientific as well as technological applications, including detection of proteins [4], photovoltaic and energy related applications [5-7], catalysis [8,9], bioimaging, biosensing etc. [10], which are discussed and reported in a large number of review articles and book chapters [11-15]. GQDs are particularly a promising nanomaterial to replace the commonly used PL inorganic QDs and fluorescent dyes for in vitro and in vivo biosensing and bioimaging related applications. However, the ability to modulate the properties of fluorescent GQDs is important for diverse biological applications. Covalent surface functionalization introduces hetero atoms, mostly O, N and S, and functional groups to the GQDs. It thus increases water solubility and other useful properties such as adsorption by biomolecules. In 2012, the properties of nitrogen doped GQDs was investigated at first by Zhao and coworkers [16]. Moreover, innanomaterial GQDs, many of the physical and chemical properties of the doped GQDs are usually determined by fabrication methods. Based on the GQDs preparation strategies developed in recent years, these methods can be classified largely into two categories: top-down and bottom-up, according to the precursor materials [17-19]. In the case of a top-down approach, splitting large 2-D carbon materials like GO, CNT, graphene, soot, coal, carbon fibers, etc. yields small 0-D GQDs. In this method, the synthesis is carried out using various approaches, including hydrothermal [20], solvothermal [21], microwave assisted, and acidic–oxidation process etc [22]. The main drawbacks in the top-down approach are less control on morphology and stability of the QDs along with infrequent use of strong acid, which can lead to the formation of toxic gases. For example, in the preparation of GQD from GO, the latter is synthesized by Hummer’s method in which acid is used with harsh oxidizing agents [23,24]. Comparing with top-down strategy, in bottom-up approach, small organic molecule are pyrolyzed together, or stepwise converted into the GQDs with superior control over morphology and stability of the QDs, but yield of GQDs are low. Generally, in bottom-up approach citric acid, ascorbic acid, sugar, glucose, sucrose etc., were used as a carbon precursor [17,25]. Recently, waste derived GQDs attracted much attention in comparison to chemically synthesized GQDs. Wang et al. synthesised RH-GQD from rice husk by treating precursors in a tube furnace at 700 °C for 2 h, and as-synthesised RH-GQD was applied for the cell imaging[26]. Wu et al. developed GQD from coffee grounds by using hydrothermal method and used for bioimaging such as detection of HeLa cells [27]. Similarly, Kalita et al. synthesized GQD from rice grain and checked their bioimaging and biocompatibility with HeLa cells [28].
On the other hand, senel et al. developed nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) from citric acid and p-aminophenol by using hydrothermal method and used for biomedical application [29].

Recently, a few published reports have described preparations of nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) for various applications including cell imaging. Hu et al. synthesized N-GQD using oxidized debris by facile hydrothermal treatment of GO and ammonia and used for bioimaging of HeLa cells .[30] Feng et al prepared N-GQD from citric acid and diethylamine, and applied it for the MCF-7 cells imaging [31]. He et al. prepared N-GQD hydrothermally from renewable abundant cow milk [32]. Liu et al. prepared N-GQD from citric acid and ammonia, and used for the detection of insecticide thiacloprid.[33] It may be pointed out that Shinde et al. prepared GQD from MWCNT [34], and, Chandra et al. modified the as-prepared GQD with polymeric material, called P-GQD, to reduce its toxicity and used in biomedical applications [35]. As mentioned GQD synthesized from bio-waste material showed good results to the bioimaging application. But they used time consuming synthesis procedure such as conventional hydrothermal method, and often prepared GQDs showed cytotoxicity to biological cells [26,27,29]. Hence, there is a need to the synthesis of bio-waste derived non-toxic GQDs by using facilemethod in a short time.

In this article, we report natural waste arjuna bark and waste materia melamine sponge, as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively, to the bottom-up strategy based preparation of non-toxic N-GQDs using facile microwave heating and employed for cell imaging and H2O2 sensing applications. The synthesized aqueous N-GQDs showed blue colored intense photoluminescence under UV light and was found to possess sizes of less than 5 nm. The as-prepared N-GQD was successfully applied for bioimaging using a model breast carcionoma cell MDA-MB-231. It may be noted that there are many reports on exploitation of GQDs as an inexpensive and useful probe to visualize biological cells by fluorescent imaging [36,37]. Further, we used fluorescent quenching behavior of the prepared N-GQD for sensing H2O2, an unique cell toxin due to its stability in abiotic environments at ambient temperature and pH, to generate highly reactive OH radicals. In the diseased animal body, H2O2 is produced mainly in three places, such as lung, gut and thyroid gland. Therefore, in-vivo sensing of produced H2O2 in human body is important [38,39]. The aqueous N-GQD was found to sense H2O2 in the concentration range of 0.1 M to 0.3 M. Further, for practical applications of both these bioimaging and biosensing studies, cytotoxicity test of the synthesized N-GQDs with human cells is an important requirement. The cell cytotoxicity of the prepared N-GQD was determined by the standard MTT assay and found non-toxic (70% survival) to the MDA-MB-231 cells, even at very high concentration (∼1.8 mg/ml) of the N-GQDs. Moreover, the effect of cell culture media on the fluorescent stability of the N-GQD was verified, which provided insights on retention of fluorescence of the prepared N-GQD under physiological environments for in vitro cell imaging and sensing. On the whole, this study implies potential applications of such non-toxic fluorescent N-GQDs in the area of cell bioimaging as it retains good fluorescence at various physiological pH conditions.

Melamine sponge and arjuna bark were procured from local market. To show the potential application of the fluorescent N-GQD as a biological probe, we performed cell imaging experiments using a human breast cancer cells line MDA-MB-231, gifted by Dr. Amit Dutt, Advanced Cancer Training Research and Education centre (ACTREC), India. For bio-compatibility evaluation with the model cell MDA-MB-231, the cytotoxicity of as-synthesized N-GQD was studied using a standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) cell viability assay, which was obtained from Thermo Fischer Scientific, India. A culture media consisting of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM): F12 was prepared and maintained to support the growth of cells line MDA-MB-231 for toxicity studies. Further, the growth media was supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic solution containing penicillin, streptomycin, and amphotericin, obtained from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India. All other used cell culture gread chemicals and solvents such as 30% H2O2, EtOH, DMSO, PFA etc. were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Mill Q water was always used for synthesis and other analyses.

The as-synthesized N-doped GQDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, which was measured on a Brucker Perkin Elmer100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were monitored by using a PHI 5000 versaprobe scanning ESCA microprobe. The morphology of the as-prepared material was examined with the help of FEG-SEM analysis (Tescan MIRA 3 model). Size and shape characterizations of the N-GQD samples were performed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, model TEM PHILIPS CM 200). The UV–vis characterization of the synthesized N-GQD was carried out by an absorption spectrophotometer (model V-750 Jasco). Further, fluorescence spectra of the prepared GQD samples were recorded by using a spectrofluorimeter (FP-8200/Jasco) .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD)
ce:section.ce:para:
The N-GQD was synthesized by a facile microwave process. 20 ml of DMSO solution was added in to 20 ml Milli-Q water and the resulting solution was sonicated for 2–3 min. Further, 0.5 gm of waste melamine sponge and 1 gm of arjuna bark were added into the as-prepared solution and mixed thoroughly by ultrasonication for about 30 min. The resultant solution was transferred into a 100 ml conical flask, which was kept inside a domestic microwave oven, and heated at a power of 700 W for 10 min. After the completion of the reaction, the flask was cooled and the resultant reaction mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The prepared solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter paper. The filtrate was then dialyzed using a dialysis bag, having cut-off molecular weight of 500 Da, for 24 h, and then the clear solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator and dried at 80 °C [40-44]. The total yield of the as-prepared N-GQD was about 72.5 % in weight [40]. The synthesis of the N-GQD is illustrated in the scheme 1
. Further, acomparative synthesis procedures with data of bio-waste derived N-GQDs with reported waste derived N-GQD are tabulated in Table S1. The as-synthesized N-GQD gave excellent water solubility such as 5 mg/ml, which is in agreement with other reported work [43].

The ground-state absorption spectroscopy study of the aqueous N-GQD samples were carried out using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo-Scientific) in an optical quartz cell of 10 mm path length, whereas steady-state (SS) fluorescence measurements were performed using a spectrofluorometer (Horiba fluoromax-4) in a 10 mm × 10 mm quartz cuvette. The aqueous solution of as-prepared N-GQD showed pale-yellow colour under visible light, but produced intense blue fluorescence under illumination of UV radiation at an excitation wavelength ∼ 365 nm. To further, understand fluorescence characteristics of the prepared N-GQD for bio-imaging of cells, we investigated its fluorescence stability under different physiological conditions and monitored using a BioTek Synergy H1 multimode plate reader.

Bio-compatibility evaluation of the prepared N-GQD was performed using standard MTT assay on model cells line MDA-MB-231 [45]. Briefly, the MDA-MB-231 cells, at 30x103 cells per well, were seeded in 96 well plates and incubated overnight in the culture media DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) supplied with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for adherence. Following day, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline, PBS (pH 7.4) and incubated with different concentration of the as-made N-GQDs, for a period of 24 h. Subsequent to the incubation, the used medium was removed and replenished with 200 µL of freshly prepared MTT (5 mg/ml) solution. After 4 hrs. incubation, the MTT medium was replaced with 200 µL DMSO to dissolve the formed blue formazan crystals. The survival rate of the cancer cells was determined by measuring the absorbance at 570 nm on the BioTek Synergy H1 (Mumbai, India) multimode plate reader while comparing it with the blank [45].

Bio-imaging permits observation and learning of biological processes in both research and clinical applications from sub-cellular to the small animal level. Therefore, bio-imaging of the as-made N-GQD, was performed on the selected MDA-MB-231 cells. Briefly, the cells, at 30x103 cells per well, were seeded in a confocal microscopy well plate, followed by incubation with the prepared N-GQD for 24 h. Post incubation, the N-GQD media solution was removed and washed with buffer PBS (pH 7.4) and imaged by a fluorescence microscope (LEICA DMI3000B, Wetzlar, Germany).

The FTIR spectral analysis was carried out to understand the functional groups attached on the surface of the N-GQD, synthesized from melamine sponge and arjuna bark. Also, the FTIR spectrum of the GQD, prepared from arjuna bark only, was obtained and analysed to identify the FTIR absorption peaks in the preferred N-GQD. In the observed FTIR spectrum Figure S1 of the GQD, a broad peak at 3390 cm−1 indicates the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [46]. Further, GQD spectrum shows absorptions due to stretching and bending vibrations of C-H bond at 2900 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1610 cm−1 points towards the formation of aromatic carbon ring [46]. Subsequently, the FTIR spectrum of the final product N-GQD was analysed to observe and verify the characteristic differences from the FTIR absorption peaks in the GQD. As shown in Fig. 1 , the observed FTIR spectrum of the N-GQD reveals a broad absorption band at 3339 cm−1 of –NH bending vibration, [47] which differs from the 3390 cm−1 peak of the GQD, pointed towards successful surface passivation by nitrogen atoms in the prepared N-GQD. Further, the observed peak at 1378 cm−1 indicates respective bending vibration mode due to C-N bonds and thus reveals the presence of C-N functional groups in the as-made N-GQD [48]. The peak at 1186 cm−1 corroborates the stretching vibration mode due to the presence of C-O bond in the N-GQD [47]. Importantly, the FTIR absorption peak at 1645 cm−1 points towards the characteristic vibration of aromatic (sp2) carbon ring in the N-GQD [47]. Therefore, these characteristic differences, along with the presence of signature vibration peaks, observed in the FTIR spectra of the prepared GQD and N-GQD, verified that N-GQD was successfully synthesized with nitrogen functionalities in it. Also, we have characterized FTIR spectra of the N-GQD after H2O2 sensing Figure S2 . Observations of characteristic FTIR peaks of the N-GQD suggests that chemical structure of the N-GQD remained stable even after interaction with H2O2.

To further understand the chemical bonding and composition on the surface of the prepared N-GQD, XPS analysis was carried out. Also, XPS analysis of the GQD, synthesized from the arjuna bark only, was obtained which showed presence of two peaks at 284 eV and 532 eV for C1s and O1s, respectively Figure S3
a. In C1s photoelectron spectrum of the GQD, we observed respective peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.1 eV, confirming the presence of C-C, C-O, C = O, and COOH bonds Figure S3
b. The O1s of the GQD spectra showed peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.8 eV for C = O and C-O, respectively (Figure S2c) [45]. On the other hand, as shown in the Fig. 2 , analysis of the XPS survey spectrum along with photoelectrons spectra of the prepared N-GQD indicates the presence of three prominent peaks around (C1s) 284 eV, (N1s) 400 eV, and (O1s) 532 eV for carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively (Fig. 2a). In C1s spectra, the observation of the peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV and 288.0 eV validated the presence of C-C, C-N/C-O, C = O and –COOH bonds, respectively Fig. 2 b. In O1s spectra, we observed the peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.8 eV for C = O and C-O, respectively Fig. 2 c. It is worth noting that oxygen functionality is important for solubility of the made N-GQD in water and other polar media. In N1s spectra of the N-GQD, peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.7 eV were observed corresponding to the respective C-N and N-H bonds Fig. 2 d.[49]

Thus, the comparative photoelectron spectral analyses of the prepared GQD and N-GQD established clearly that N- atom was doped successfully on the surfaces of the as-prepared N-GQD. Notably, the presence of these functional groups makes the synthesized N-GQDs well soluble in water, improves adsorbing ability to biomolecules leading to potential application for bioimaging. The estimated doping of nitrogen in the as-made N-GQDs was about 13.7 % in weight, by its XPS analysis and is tabulated in Table S2 [49].

Moreover, TEM analysis was carried out to understand the morphology of the as-prepared N-GQD. The TEM images are shown in Fig. 3 which reveals the shape and size distribution of the N-GQDs. The TEM images points towards highly crystalline spherical nature of the synthesized N-GQDs and its average diameter is 2–3 nm.

To understand the photoluminescent behaviour of the prepared N-GQD, its aqueous PBS (pH 7.4) solution was subjected to UV–vis absorption and emission spectroscopy studies. As shown in Fig. 4 , the UV–vis spectrum of the N-GQD showed an intense absorption peak at ∼ 220 nm with a tail extending to visible spectral range. The strong UV peak (220 nm) is attributed to the π-π* transition from the formed sp2 bonded carbons sheet in the graphene core. In addition, the absorption shoulders in the visible region 300 nm to 700 nm may be due to π-π* and n-π* absorptions of the C = C, C = O bonds, and other chemical functionalities including non-bonding electrons in the dopant N, O atoms [12,13]. Importantly, the fluorescence

Spectrum of the aqueous N-GQD is found to be independent of any excitation wavelength within 395 nm to 405 nm of its absorption profile which gave a characteristic broad emission with a peak at ∼ 495 nm, as shown in Fig. 4. The relative quantum yield of fluorescence (QYF) of the prepared aqueous N-GQD in this work was 8.5 %. The QYF of N-GQD was comparatively determined by using quinine sulphate (QYF = 0.54 in water with 0.5 M H2SO4) as the standard sample at excitation wavelength 310 nm and is calculated by using the following standard Equation (1) [49].
(1)
Q s
=
Q r
A r
/
A s
I s
/
I r
η s
/
η r
2

Where ‘QS’ is the QYF, ‘I’ is the observed integrated emission intensity, ‘A’ is the absorbance of the solution at the excitation wavelength, and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘s’ and ‘r’ refer to the unknown and standard fluorophore, respectively.

Generally, GQDs with planner structure show dominant π-π* transition. The as-prepared N-GQDs with oxygen rich functional groups gave blue colored luminescence with maximum near 495 nm. The functionalization of nitrogen helps GQDs in delocalization of the π-electrons to an additional extent making the band-gap transition excited N-GQDs emit in blue region of the visible spectrum The efficient blue emission results possibly from the complex interactions between the higher electronegative dopant atoms, N and O bonded to highly conjugated aromatic sp2 carbons, which induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the size specific GQDs [32,50].

The observation of excitation independent fluorescence spectrum of the as-made N-GQD may indicate that both the size and surface state of these sp2 sheets contained in the N-GQD are fairly uniform [12,13]. To recognize the use of this prepared fluorescent N-GQD, its fluorescence properties under pertinent physiological environments of the biological cells culture were investigated further, which are discussed in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence of N-GQD at physiological pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To further, understand the biological usefulness of the fluorescent N-GQD, we studied its fluorescence stability under different pH conditions of the cell culture. The aqueous PBS (pH 7.4) solution of the N-GQD, excited at 405 nm, showed a little modulation of its fluorescence intensity depending on different pH conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous N-GQD at pH 7.4 is found to increase with higher pH value up to 9, and decreased with lower pH value up to 4, which could be ascribed to protonation and de-protonation of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-GQD.

However, fluorescence spectra of the N-GQD were observed to maintain its emission peak wavelength primarily at ∼495 nm in the studied range of pH 4 to 9. In the neutral pH range 7–7.4, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQD was almost unchanged. Further, it is to be noted here that the prepared N-GQD exhibited retention of its fluorescence intensity, with a marginal decrease even at high acidic condition of pH 4, and showed increased fluorescence intensity at alkaline pH 9. This result implies potential applications of the prepared N-GQD in the area of biosensors and bioimaging as it shows good fluorescence at various pH conditions [51].

Further, we investigated the effect of hydrogen peroxide, a common reactive oxygen species in biological systems, on the fluorescence of the aqueous PBS (pH 7.4) N-GQD. As shown in Fig. 6
a fluorescence of the N-GQD was found to quench strongly in presence of 0.1 M to 1 M concentration of hydrogen peroxide, while lower concentration range (up to 0.3 M) of H2O2 showed a small and systematic decrease in its fluorescence intensity. The Fig. 6 b shows a plot on the linearity of peak fluorescence intensity of the aqueous (pH 7.4) N-GQD towards H2O2 in the concentration range up to 0.3 M with linear regression equation. Limit of detection (LOD) of H2O2, in this range of H2O2 concentration, was calculated according to 3.3*σ/s, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements, ‘s’ is the slope of the linear calibration curve. The calculated LOD of H2O2 is found to be 0.212 M, by interaction with the N-GQD at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. Such detection of H2O2 in a large dynamic range, up to 300 mM, by the prepared N-GQD may be potentially useful for animal cells physiological and pathological studies. A high concentration of H2O2 would probably increase collisional interactions with the N-GQDs that may change its surface states, leading to significant reduction in fluorescence intensity of the latter.

It is suggested that in presence of these QDs, H2O2 may be oxidized to form H2O and O2, which is schematically shown in Figure S4 . Usually, such fluorescence quenching of the GQDs occurs due to either energy transfer or surface absorption, [27] which could change the surface states of the N-GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence of N-GQD at cell culture environment
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further understand the biological usefulness of the fluorescent N-GQD, its fluorescence stability was investigated under different conditions of the cell culture. We studied the effects of physiological media and serum on fluorescence spectra of the aqueous N-GQD at pH 7.4 (in PBS), which are shown in Figures S5 and S6 . Addition of DMEM, a widely used basal medium for supporting growth of the cells, in the aqueous N-GQD decreased its fluorescence intensity marginally, without a shift in the peak emission wavelength, even in the presence of high concentration,80% with respect to the QD, of DMEM Figure S5 . Similar insignificant decrease in fluorescence intensity of the aqueous N-GQD at pH 7.4, without much shift in its emission peak, was observed in presence of the fixing agents such as ethanol and paraformaldehyde (PFA) at different volume ratio Figures S6 and S7 . It is to be pointed out that fluorescence intensity of the N-GQD was observed to show a marginal reduction, in presence of increasing volume ratio of the respective solvent component of EtOH and PFA. These studies indicates suitability of the as-prepared N-GQD to be used for bioimaging at physiological conditions.

Since the aqueous fluorescent N-GQD showed stable emission wavelength peak and fairly good fluorescence intensity under different physiological conditions, it was tested further for in vitro bioimaging of the human cells. It is to be noted that the bioimaging process has an important role in both research and clinical applications and allows intrinsic observations of biological processes from sub cellular to animal. However, an intended fluorescent probe for biological applications, such as bioimaging and biosensing, should not only possess good fluorescence intensity under the physiological conditions but also needed to offer low toxicity and good biocompatibility with biological systems. Thus, to understand the potential application of the as-made N-GQD as a biological fluorescent probe, its in vitro cell cytotoxicity was performed by an MTT assay using the selected MDA MB 231 (breast cancer) cell line as a model. The percentage survival rate of the cancer cells was determined by measuring the ratio of the absorbance at 570 nm of the cells incubated with the N-GQDs to that of the cells incubated with blank culture medium (DMEM) only on a BioTek multimode plate reader.

The survival rate of the cells was determined after exposing these cells with the N-GQD stepwise up to maximum 4.5 mg/ml concentration for a period of 24 h. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 7
, the tested carcinoma cells show more than 70% survivability even in presence of high concentration (1.8 mg/ml) of the N-GQD, suggesting its potential use as an effective bioimaging probe with good biocompatibility even at its high concentration.

It may be noted that various literature reports suggested use of GQD concentration up ∼100 µg/ml for bioimaging study. Most importantly, using the present N-GQD as a fluorescent probe, the uptake and bioimaging experiments of the model cancer cells were successfully studied in-vitro, conducted by a confocal fluorescence microscope. After being incubated with the N-GQD (1 mg/ml) for a period of 24 h, the bright-field and fluorescence images of the cells were separately recorded. As depicted in the Fig. 8 , blue fluorescent cells were observed, after incubation for a period of 24 h with the N-GQD, when excited with a laser at 405 nm, showing the locations of the cells brightly by internalized N-GQD fluorescence which appeared evenly distributed. The prepared N-GQDs could probably penetrate into the cells nuclei due to their very small size (2–3 nm) and good adherence. The entire cell body showed the bright fluorescence and no distinct difference was observed between cytoplasm and nuclei, which indicates that N-GQD was efficiently internalized and picked up homogeniously by all the cells with almost equal brightness [52,53]. On the other hand, no visible fluorescence was noticed in the control sample without the prepared N-GQD under similar conditions. These in vitro studies, revealing excellent biocompatibility as well as imaging of the model cancer cells, confirmed that the prepared non-toxic fluorescent N-GQD might have potential applications towards in.vivo cell bioimaging.

In summary, we have developed water soluble, fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) from inexhaustible precursors using a facile method for bioimaging and biosensing applications. The N-GQD was synthesized using waste derived sources, arjuna bark and melamine sponge, by microwave treatment. The prepared N-GQD gave an intense absorbance peak at ∼220 nm with absorption shoulder extending to visible range, and exhibited strong blue coloured fluorescence (QYF ∼ 8.5 %) with peak at ∼495 nm, when excited at ∼400 nm. The as-prepared aqueous N-GQD, having diameter 2–3 nm, was successfully applied for the in vitro bioimaging study conducted on MDA MB 231 cell line (human breast carcinoma). Importantly, the cells showed more than 70% survivability even for using high concentration (1.8 mg/ml) of the N-GQD. These results indicated that the prepared N-GQD is an excellent fluorescent probe to carry out bioimaging for in vivo biological cells. Moreover, the prepared N-GQD showed usefulness in sensing H2O2, a generated toxin in the animal cells. Overall, this study helps to synthesize from abundant waste a fluorescent, non-toxic nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQD), which has the potential to be a useful probe for in vitro and in vivo cell imaging and H2O2 sensing in biological systems.

Rahul V. Khose: Data Curation, Formal analysis, Writing original draft. Prachi Bangde: Data Curation, Writing original draft. Mahesh P. Bondarde: Formal analysis, Writing original draft. Pratik S. Dhumal: Formal analysis. Madhuri A. Bhakare: Formal analysis. Goutam Chakraborty: Data Curation. Alok K. Ray: Writing original draft. Prajakta Dandekar: Data Curation. Surajit Some: Supervision, Writing original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by DAE-BRNS research grant fund (34/14/14/2015/BRNS) and CSIR (22/0748/17/EMR-II), and Chhatrapati Shahu Maharaj Research, Training and Human Development Institute (SARTHI), Pune. R.V.K is thankful to DAE-BRNS for providing research fellowship. The authors are thankful to laboratory supports of the host institutes ICT and BARC, Mumbai, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
